title: Payment System — System Design
description: Designing a payment system where correctness beats availability: a service that charges, refunds, and pays out money without ever losing or double-counting it. We build from a naive synchronous charge toward an idempotent, double-entry, reconcilable system, treating money movement as the hardest kind of distributed state to get right.

functional: Charge a customer a specified amount in a specified currency for an order.
Refund a charge fully or partially.
Record every money movement in an auditable ledger.
Pay out collected balances to merchants.
Expose payment status and merchant balance.
Process asynchronous results (authorizations, settlements, disputes) from external PSPs.
nonFunctional: Correctness first: never lose money, never double-charge, never double-pay.
Exactly-once effect on the ledger even when clients and PSPs retry.
Durable and auditable: every state change is recorded immutably.
Strong consistency for balances; availability may be sacrificed to preserve correctness.
Reconcilable: internal records must be provably consistent with external PSP records.
Idempotent APIs so safe retries never produce duplicate effects.

capacity.label: Charge throughput
capacity.note: Flash sales and billing cycles spike well above average.

capacity.label: Ledger writes
capacity.note: Double-entry: each money movement writes at least two balanced rows.

capacity.label: Status / balance reads
capacity.note: Served from a read model, not by scanning the ledger.

capacity.label: Ledger growth
capacity.note: Append-only; never updated or deleted — sized for years of retention.

capacity.label: Reconciliation window
capacity.note: Compare internal ledger against PSP settlement files.

capacity.label: Durability target
capacity.note: Money records are the source of truth; replicated and backed up.

api.description: Create (authorize+capture or authorize-only) a payment. Requires an Idempotency-Key header so retries are safe.
Refund a captured payment, fully or partially. Also idempotent.
Fetch a payment's current status and history.
Return a merchant's available and pending balance, computed from the ledger read model.
Inbound endpoint for asynchronous PSP events (settlement, dispute, late failure). Verified by signature and deduplicated by event id.

dataModel.name: accounts
dataModel.note: One row per ledger account (merchant balance, PSP clearing, fees, customer receivable). Balances are derived from entries, not stored here as truth.

dataModel.name: ledger_entries
dataModel.note: Immutable, append-only double-entry rows. Every transaction inserts >= 2 rows whose signed amounts sum to zero. Never updated or deleted.

dataModel.name: payments
dataModel.note: The lifecycle state machine of one payment. Points at the ledger transactions it produced.

dataModel.name: idempotency_keys
dataModel.note: Deduplicates client and webhook retries. Stores the first response so retries replay it instead of re-executing.

dataModel.name: outbox
dataModel.note: Events written in the same DB transaction as the state change, then relayed to the queue exactly once (transactional outbox).

patterns.name: Idempotency key
patterns.what: A client-supplied key that lets the server detect and dedupe retries, executing the effect at most once and replaying the original response.
patterns.whenToUse: Any state-changing API over an unreliable network where the client may retry — especially money movement.

patterns.name: Double-entry ledger
patterns.what: Record every money movement as balanced debit/credit entries that sum to zero, append-only and immutable.
patterns.whenToUse: Whenever correctness and auditability of balances matter more than write convenience.

patterns.name: Transactional outbox
patterns.what: Write domain events to an outbox table in the same DB transaction as the state change, then relay them to a queue, so state and events can't diverge.
patterns.whenToUse: When you must publish an event exactly-once alongside a database write without distributed transactions.

patterns.name: Saga / state machine
patterns.what: Model a multi-step money flow as explicit states with compensating actions instead of one big distributed transaction.
patterns.whenToUse: Long-running flows spanning external systems (authorize → capture → settle) that can fail partway.

patterns.name: Reconciliation
patterns.what: Periodically compare your internal records against an authoritative external source and surface any drift for repair.
patterns.whenToUse: Any integration where a second system also holds the truth (PSPs, banks) and the two can diverge.

patterns.name: Read model (CQRS)
patterns.what: Derive a fast, query-optimized view (balances) from the authoritative write store (the ledger) rather than querying it directly.
patterns.whenToUse: Read-heavy queries over an append-only or normalized write store that's expensive to aggregate on the fly.

patterns.name: Sharding by key
patterns.what: Partition the ledger by account so writes scale horizontally while keeping a transaction's entries co-located.
patterns.whenToUse: When ledger write volume outgrows a single primary.

steps.title: 1. Synchronous Charge (the naive baseline)
steps.description: Start with the simplest thing that could work: the client calls the payment service, which calls the external PSP and returns the result in one synchronous request. This exposes the core problem before we add machinery.
steps.decisionPrompt: What goes wrong the first time this request times out or the client retries?

concepts.definition: An external provider (e.g. card networks via a gateway) that actually moves money.
concepts.whyItMatters: It's a third party you can't transactionally couple to — the source of most correctness hazards.

concepts.definition: Authorization reserves funds; capture actually moves them.
concepts.whyItMatters: Many flows authorize now and capture later, which forces asynchronous design.

talkingPoints: Name the read:write ratio and that correctness dominates here, unlike most read-heavy designs.
Point out immediately that a network timeout makes the client unsure whether money moved.

traps.trap: Treating the PSP call as if it were a local, transactional database write
traps.why: You cannot roll back an external charge with your DB transaction; a crash after charging leaves money moved but no record.
traps.instead: Design for the in-doubt case from the start: record intent before calling the PSP, and reconcile afterward.

bottlenecks.issue: A timeout leaves the outcome unknown — retrying may double-charge.
bottlenecks.mitigation: Introduce idempotency keys (next step) so retries are safe.

strong: Spots the double-charge-on-retry hazard without being prompted.
Frames money movement as 'at-most-once effect over an unreliable dependency'.
weak: Assumes the PSP call always returns cleanly.
Reaches for distributed transactions across the PSP.

whyNow: A concrete baseline makes every later addition a response to a specific failure, not abstract best practice.

steps.title: 2. Idempotency Keys (safe retries)
steps.description: Add a client-supplied Idempotency-Key. The service claims the key before doing work; if the same key arrives again, it replays the stored response instead of charging twice. This converts an unreliable network into at-most-once effects.
steps.decisionPrompt: Where do you store the key, and what do you do when a retry arrives while the first request is still in flight?

concepts.definition: An operation that has the same effect whether applied once or many times.
concepts.whyItMatters: It's the only safe way to let clients retry money movement.

concepts.definition: At-least-once delivery (retries) plus dedupe (idempotency) yields one effect.
concepts.whyItMatters: True exactly-once delivery is impossible; exactly-once effect is achievable and is what we actually need.

options.name: Key row claimed in the payments DB (default)
pros: Same transactional store as the payment — claim and result commit together.
No extra system to operate or keep consistent.
cons: Couples idempotency throughput to the primary DB.

options.name: Dedicated key store (e.g. Redis with persistence)
pros: Fast claims, isolates idempotency load from the payments DB.
cons: Two stores can diverge on a crash between claim and commit — needs careful recovery.

flows.name: Retry replays the stored response
flows.note: The second request with the same key never reaches the PSP.

traps.why: If the server makes the key, a client retry gets a new key and charges again — the dedupe is defeated.
traps.instead: The client generates the key once per logical attempt and reuses it across retries.

traps.trap: Checking the key but not locking it, so concurrent retries both proceed
traps.why: Two in-flight requests with the same key can both pass the check and both charge.
traps.instead: Claim the key atomically (unique insert / SELECT FOR UPDATE) so only one request executes; others wait or replay.

failureDrills.scenario: Crash after claiming the key but before charging the PSP.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The key is left 'in_progress'; a retry must not assume success.
failureDrills.mitigation: On retry of an in_progress key, re-drive the operation safely (the PSP call is itself idempotent via the same key) rather than returning success blindly.

strong: Distinguishes exactly-once delivery (impossible) from exactly-once effect (the goal).
Handles the concurrent-retry race, not just the sequential one.
weak: Thinks a simple 'has this key been seen' check is sufficient.
Ignores the in-flight window between claim and completion.

recap.after: Retries are safe — at most one charge per key.
recap.newRisk: An in-progress key after a crash needs careful, idempotent recovery.

whyNow: Every later component (capture, webhooks) also retries, so idempotency must exist before we add asynchrony.

steps.title: 3. Double-Entry Ledger (the source of truth)
steps.description: Replace ad-hoc balance columns with an append-only, immutable double-entry ledger. Every money movement writes balanced entries that sum to zero across accounts. Balances become a function of the ledger, not a mutable field that can drift.
steps.decisionPrompt: Why is an append-only ledger safer than incrementing a balance column, and what invariant must every write preserve?

concepts.definition: Each transaction records equal and opposite entries (debit one account, credit another).
concepts.whyItMatters: The zero-sum invariant makes lost or invented money detectable by construction.

concepts.definition: Entries are never updated or deleted; corrections are new compensating entries.
concepts.whyItMatters: An immutable log is auditable and replayable — essential for money.

deepDives.title: The balanced-entry invariant
points: A transaction inserts >= 2 rows sharing a transaction_id; their signed amounts MUST sum to zero.
Enforce it in code and verify it in reconciliation — a non-zero sum is a bug or corruption.
A refund is not an update: it's a new transaction with reversing entries.

traps.trap: Storing the balance as a mutable integer column and incrementing it
traps.why: Concurrent updates race, and a lost update silently loses money with no audit trail.
traps.instead: Make balance a derived sum of immutable ledger entries; never mutate it in place.

traps.trap: Editing or deleting a wrong entry
traps.why: Mutating history destroys auditability and breaks reconciliation against external records.
traps.instead: Append a compensating (reversing) entry so the full history is preserved.

failureDrills.scenario: A bug posts only one side of a transaction.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: The transaction's entries no longer sum to zero.
failureDrills.mitigation: Write all entries of a transaction atomically; reconciliation flags any transaction_id whose entries don't net to zero.

strong: Treats balances as derived, not stored.
Models refunds as new reversing transactions, not edits.
weak: Keeps a mutable balance column as the source of truth.
Proposes updating ledger rows to fix mistakes.

recap.before: Balances were mutable fields that could drift or race.
recap.after: Balances are provable sums over an immutable, auditable ledger.
recap.newRisk: Reads that aggregate the whole ledger get slow — addressed by a read model later.

whyNow: Before adding asynchronous flows that post money from several places, we need one authoritative, tamper-evident record.

steps.title: 4. Asynchronous Capture & State Machine
steps.description: Decouple authorization from capture. The payment becomes an explicit state machine (authorized → capturing → captured / failed), and a capture worker drives it via a queue. Events are published using a transactional outbox so state and events never diverge.
steps.decisionPrompt: How do you publish a 'payment authorized' event and write the payment state without a distributed transaction?

concepts.definition: A sequence of local transactions with compensating actions instead of one global transaction.
concepts.whyItMatters: You can't hold a transaction across the PSP, so failures are handled by compensation, not rollback.

concepts.definition: Events written to an outbox table in the same DB transaction as the state change, then relayed to the queue.
concepts.whyItMatters: Guarantees the event is published if and only if the state change committed.

options.name: Transactional outbox + relay (default)
pros: Exactly-once publish relative to the state change.
No distributed transaction; just one local DB transaction plus an at-least-once relay (consumers dedupe).
cons: Relay adds a moving part; outbox table needs pruning.

options.name: Dual write (publish to queue, then write DB)
cons: Classic dual-write hazard: a crash between the two leaves state and events inconsistent.
Can drop events or emit phantom ones — unacceptable for money.

flows.name: Authorize now, capture asynchronously
flows.note: The client gets a fast 'authorized'; capture and ledger posting happen off the request path.

traps.trap: Dual-writing to the queue and the database
traps.why: If one succeeds and the other fails, state and events diverge — you lose or invent money events.
traps.instead: Use a transactional outbox so the event commits atomically with the state change.

traps.trap: Assuming the capture worker runs exactly once
traps.why: Queues redeliver; a naive worker captures or posts to the ledger twice.
traps.instead: Make capture idempotent (keyed by payment_id + step) so redelivery is a no-op.

failureDrills.scenario: Capture worker crashes after capturing at the PSP but before posting to the ledger.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: On redelivery it must post the ledger entries exactly once, not re-capture.
failureDrills.mitigation: Idempotent steps keyed per (payment_id, phase); the PSP capture is itself idempotent and the ledger post is deduped.

strong: Reaches for the outbox to avoid dual writes.
Makes every async consumer idempotent because queues redeliver.
weak: Publishes to the queue and writes the DB as two independent steps.

recap.before: Charge was one synchronous all-or-nothing call.
recap.after: Payment is an explicit state machine driven asynchronously with reliable events.
recap.newRisk: More moving parts; consumers must be idempotent and the outbox must be relayed and pruned.

whyNow: Real PSP flows authorize then capture later, and we want fast client responses — both demand asynchrony done safely.

steps.title: 5. Webhooks & Exactly-Once Inbound Events
steps.description: PSPs report settlements, disputes, and late failures asynchronously via webhooks. We verify signatures, deduplicate by event id, and enqueue for processing — applying the same exactly-once-effect discipline to events we receive.
steps.decisionPrompt: A PSP delivers the same webhook three times. How do you ensure the ledger reflects it once?

concepts.definition: An inbound HTTP callback the PSP sends when an event occurs.
concepts.whyItMatters: Settlement and disputes are asynchronous; webhooks are how the outside world updates our state.

concepts.definition: PSPs retry webhooks until acked, so duplicates are normal.
concepts.whyItMatters: We must dedupe on our side; we can't ask the PSP to deliver exactly once.

flows.name: Duplicate webhook is a no-op
flows.note: Signature verified first; event_id dedupe makes reprocessing safe.

traps.trap: Acting on a webhook before verifying its signature
traps.why: Anyone who learns the URL could forge settlement or refund events and move money.
traps.instead: Verify the PSP signature first; reject anything that doesn't validate.

traps.trap: Processing webhooks synchronously inside the HTTP handler
traps.why: Slow ledger work makes you miss the PSP's ack deadline, triggering even more retries.
traps.instead: Verify + dedupe + enqueue quickly, ack immediately, and process off the request path.

failureDrills.scenario: A webhook arrives for a payment we have no record of (out-of-order delivery).
failureDrills.mitigation: Persist the event and reconcile later, or hold it for retry until the corresponding payment record exists.

strong: Verifies signatures before any side effect.
Treats inbound events with the same idempotency rigor as outbound calls.
weak: Does heavy work before acking, inviting retry storms.

recap.before: We only knew outcomes we synchronously observed.
recap.after: Asynchronous PSP outcomes update our state exactly once.
recap.newRisk: Inbound events can be forged, duplicated, or arrive out of order — all handled explicitly.

whyNow: Settlement and disputes are inherently asynchronous; without webhooks our state silently lags reality.

steps.title: 6. Reconciliation Against the PSP
steps.description: The PSP is a second source of truth. A reconciliation service ingests PSP settlement files and compares them against our ledger, surfacing any drift (missing, extra, or mismatched amounts) for automated or manual repair.
steps.decisionPrompt: Your ledger says you collected $1,000,000 today; the PSP file says $999,300. What do you do?

concepts.definition: Comparing internal records to an authoritative external statement to detect and resolve differences.
concepts.whyItMatters: Two systems holding money records will drift; reconciliation is how you catch it before it compounds.

concepts.definition: A line item that doesn't match between the two sources.
concepts.whyItMatters: Breaks are the unit of work for finance ops; the system should minimize and route them.

deepDives.title: How a break is resolved
points: Auto-match by PSP reference; classify unmatched lines as missing-in-ledger or missing-in-PSP.
Some breaks are timing (settles tomorrow) and clear themselves; others need a compensating ledger entry.
Never silently edit history — corrections are new entries with a reason code.

traps.trap: Assuming the ledger is right and the PSP is wrong (or vice versa)
traps.why: Either side can be wrong; guessing hides real money loss.
traps.instead: Treat a break as an investigation; resolve with an auditable correcting entry once the truth is known.

traps.trap: Reconciling only totals, not line items
traps.why: Two offsetting errors can net to a matching total while individual payments are wrong.
traps.instead: Reconcile at the transaction grain, then roll up.

failureDrills.scenario: The PSP file is late or partial.
failureDrills.expectedBehavior: Reconciliation doesn't falsely report mass breaks.
failureDrills.mitigation: Track file completeness/sequence; defer reconciliation for the missing window rather than flagging everything.

strong: Reconciles at line-item grain and distinguishes timing breaks from real ones.
Resolves breaks with compensating entries, preserving history.
weak: Edits ledger rows to make numbers match.

recap.before: We trusted that our ledger matched the PSP.
recap.after: Drift between our records and the PSP is detected and repaired with an audit trail.
recap.newRisk: Break volume can grow; ops tooling and auto-matching keep it manageable.

whyNow: Once money flows through an external party, 'probably consistent' isn't good enough — we must prove it daily.

steps.title: 7. Payouts & Balance Read Model
steps.description: Merchants need their money and fast balance reads. We derive a balance read model from the ledger (CQRS) for 50k rps lookups, and a payout service moves available balances to bank rails — itself idempotent and ledger-recorded.
steps.decisionPrompt: Why not compute balances by summing the ledger on every read, and how do you keep the read model correct?

concepts.definition: A separate, query-optimized projection of the write store (here: a per-account running balance).
concepts.whyItMatters: Summing an append-only ledger per read doesn't scale to 50k rps.

concepts.definition: Pending funds are authorized/settling; available funds can be paid out.
concepts.whyItMatters: Paying out pending money risks clawbacks on disputes or failed settlement.

flows.name: Payout draws only available balance
flows.note: Pending funds are excluded so disputes can't create a negative balance.

traps.trap: Paying out the pending balance, not just available
traps.why: A later dispute or failed settlement claws back funds you already sent, leaving a negative balance.
traps.instead: Only pay out funds that have settled and cleared the dispute window.

traps.trap: Letting the read model be the source of truth
traps.why: A projection bug then becomes lost money with no recovery.
traps.instead: Keep the ledger authoritative; the read model is rebuildable by replaying entries.

failureDrills.scenario: The balance read model lags the ledger.
failureDrills.mitigation: Payout re-checks against the authoritative ledger transactionally before transferring; the read model is for fast reads, not the final guard.

strong: Keeps the ledger authoritative and the read model rebuildable.
Separates available from pending to prevent clawback negatives.
weak: Computes balances by scanning the ledger per request.

recap.before: Balance reads would scan the whole ledger; no payout path.
recap.after: Fast balance reads from a projection, and safe idempotent payouts.
recap.newRisk: The read model can lag or have bugs — it must be rebuildable and never the final authority.

whyNow: With money correctly recorded, merchants need to see and withdraw it without overloading the ledger.

steps.title: 8. Scaling the Ledger (sharding)
steps.description: Ledger write volume outgrows one primary. Partition by account so each transaction's entries stay co-located on one shard, preserving atomic balanced writes while scaling horizontally. Cross-account transfers need explicit handling.
steps.decisionPrompt: If you shard the ledger by account, what happens to a transaction that touches two accounts on different shards?

concepts.definition: The attribute (here account_id) that decides which shard a row lives on.
concepts.whyItMatters: Choosing account keeps a single account's entries together for fast, consistent reads.

concepts.definition: Keeping related rows on the same shard so they can be written in one local transaction.
concepts.whyItMatters: Balanced double-entry writes are easiest when both sides live on one shard.

options.name: Shard by account, clearing account per shard (default)
pros: Most transactions (charge merchant vs. a per-shard clearing account) stay on one shard.
cons: Cross-shard transfers need a two-entry saga: post on each shard with a linking transaction_id, reconciled for completeness.

options.name: Single primary, scale vertically + read replicas
pros: Simplest: all balanced writes are trivially local.
cons: Write ceiling of one machine; eventually the bottleneck returns.

deepDives.title: Cross-shard transactions
points: Keep entries balanced per transaction_id even when split across shards.
Use a saga: write the debit on shard A and the credit on shard B, both tagged with the same transaction_id.
Reconciliation verifies that every transaction_id nets to zero across shards — a missing side is a detectable break.

traps.why: A single account's entries scatter across shards, making balance reads and per-account consistency expensive.
traps.instead: Shard by account so an account's history and balance live together.

traps.why: There's no free distributed transaction; a naive two-shard write can leave one side missing.
traps.instead: Use a saga with a shared transaction_id and let reconciliation catch incomplete transactions.

strong: Picks account as the shard key to co-locate balanced entries.
Handles cross-shard transfers as a reconciled saga, not a fake distributed transaction.
weak: Shards by a key that scatters one account's entries.

recap.before: Ledger writes were capped by a single primary.
recap.after: Writes scale horizontally while balanced entries stay co-located per account.
recap.newRisk: Cross-shard transfers become sagas that reconciliation must verify.

whyNow: At ~4k ledger writes/sec growing daily, one primary is the next hard bottleneck.

steps.title: 8a. Where Money Demands Strong Consistency
steps.description: Be deliberate about consistency. Ledger writes and payout guards need strong consistency; balance reads and analytics can tolerate lag. Spend the consistency budget where wrong answers move money.
steps.decisionPrompt: Which operations may return slightly stale data, and which must never?

concepts.definition: Strong: reads see the latest committed write. Eventual: reads may lag but converge.
concepts.whyItMatters: Money correctness needs strong guarantees on the critical path; not everywhere, or you pay needless latency/availability.

traps.why: You pay latency and availability for reads (analytics, dashboards) where staleness is harmless.
traps.instead: Strong consistency on ledger writes and payout guards; eventual for derived reads.

strong: Allocates the consistency budget by blast radius of a wrong answer.
Re-validates against the ledger on the payout critical path.
weak: Applies one consistency level to the whole system.

recap.after: Consistency is chosen per operation by how much a wrong answer costs.
recap.newRisk: Mislabeling a critical read as eventual would let stale data move money — call them out explicitly.

finalDesign.title: Final Design
finalDesign.description: End-to-end: idempotent APIs, a sharded append-only double-entry ledger as the source of truth, asynchronous capture via outbox + queue, signature-verified deduplicated webhooks, daily reconciliation against the PSP, a balance read model, and idempotent payouts.

functional.requirement: Charge a customer for an order
functional.how: Idempotent POST /payments authorizes and captures via the PSP and posts balanced ledger entries.
Refunds are new reversing ledger transactions, never edits to history.
An append-only double-entry ledger where balances are derived sums.
Idempotent payout service draws only available balance and records the transfer in the ledger.
Status from the payments state machine; balance from a CQRS read model projected off the ledger.
Signature-verified, deduplicated webhooks enqueue work processed exactly-once.

nonFunctional.how: Strong consistency on the ledger write path and payout guard; the system declines rather than risk double-spend.
Client idempotency keys plus idempotent async consumers and deduped webhooks.
Immutable append-only ledger, replicated and reconcilable; corrections are new entries.
Daily line-item reconciliation surfaces and repairs any drift with audit trails.
Ledger sharded by account; reads served from a projection.

say: Restate it: move money for charges, refunds, and payouts without ever losing or double-counting it.
Say the headline tradeoff out loud: correctness beats availability — unusual versus typical read-heavy designs.
List functional needs and the non-functional priorities (exactly-once effect, auditability, reconcilability).
Size charge tps, ledger writes (2x), balance reads, and ledger growth/day.
Sketch idempotent POST /payments and /refunds, GET balance, and the inbound webhook.
Start with the naive synchronous charge and immediately name the double-charge-on-retry hazard.
Add idempotency, then the double-entry ledger, then async capture via outbox, then webhooks.
Go deep on whichever the interviewer pushes: exactly-once, the ledger invariant, or reconciliation.
Cover reconciliation against the PSP and how breaks are resolved with compensating entries.
Summarize how each requirement is met and name the consistency budget explicitly.

expectations: Designs idempotent charge/refund APIs and explains why retries must be safe.
Knows a ledger should record movements rather than mutating a balance column.
Drives the double-entry invariant and exactly-once-effect reasoning.
Uses a transactional outbox to avoid dual writes; makes async consumers idempotent.
Designs reconciliation at line-item grain with compensating corrections.
Allocates the consistency budget deliberately (strong on writes/payout, eventual on reads).
Shards the ledger by account and handles cross-shard transfers as reconciled sagas.
Quantifies failure modes and frames money loss as the metric that dominates all others.

followUps: How would you handle multi-currency charges and FX, and where does the rate get fixed?
How do disputes and chargebacks flow through the ledger weeks after the original charge?
How would you support partial captures and multiple captures against one authorization?
What's your strategy for a PSP outage — queue and retry, fail closed, or fail over to a second PSP?
How do you prevent and detect internal fraud given the system can move money?
How would you rebuild the balance read model from scratch, and how long would it take at this scale?
